author: Jackstar
title: The Internet: An imposition.
content_raw:
# The Internet: An imposition.
Ah, the internet! The land of endless knowledge, entertainment, and... oh wait, who’s this little fella? A pop-up ad for something I absolutely don’t need? And *another one*? And another one? **Welcome to the digital jungle, where the mosquitoes are pop-up ads and the bug spray is your trusty ad-blocker.** 
## The *Unbeatable* Online Experience
Picture this: You’re just trying to read an article on how to relax, because life’s been stressful. Maybe it’s work, maybe it’s just the fact that *the internet is a chaotic nightmare*. But before you even get to the first tip on “how to calm down,” you’re assaulted by ads for stress-relief products. **Oh, the irony is so thick you could slice it with a knife.** Really, the universe is sending you *all the signals*, huh? “Relax, but here’s a shopping ad for ‘relaxation’ products you don’t want, but will probably buy anyway.”
And don’t even get me started on video ads. You click on a 5-minute video to, I don’t know, *learn* something. But instead, you’re forced to watch a *30-second* ad about a product you’ve *never* asked for, nor will you ever need. **It’s like being promised a slice of pizza, but instead, you’re served a side of broccoli.** And the best part? The skip button is either *invisible*, or it only shows up at the *last possible moment*, just when you’ve forgotten what you even clicked on the video for. Pure bliss.
## A New Era of Ads: More *Intrusive* Than Ever
By now, you’d think that all this technological wizardry would lead to less annoying ads. But nope! Ads have evolved. They've taken a quantum leap into being even *more* intrusive. **They're like that one guy who shows up at every party uninvited and then insists on being the life of the party**- even though you didn’t send him an invitation. Ever.In fact, global ad spending in 2025 reached a mind-boggling **$1 trillion**! {color:#ff6347}[^1]{/color} That’s a whole lot of cash for stuff we didn’t sign up for, isn’t it? The funny part? **Ads are now more personalized** than ever. They know everything: what you’re buying, what you’re searching for, what you’re thinking about, and *probably* what you dream about at night. (I mean, not yet, but give it time.) It’s like having a *creepy stalker* who knows your every move. "Oh, you were thinking about buying shoes? Here, let me remind you about it... for the 43rd time today."
## The Myth of "Supporting Free Content"  
Here’s the classic argument: "But ads are necessary to support free content!" Sure. Let’s just pretend for a moment that ads aren’t the equivalent of the obnoxious guy who insists on loudly talking during your favorite movie. **If your business model is *entirely* reliant on making people watch ads to survive, maybe it’s time to rethink your entire existence**. 
A recent study by Blockthrough revealed that **56% of consumers will immediately leave a website or close an app because of bad ads**. {color:#ff6347}[^2]{/color} Let’s be honest: If I wanted to watch commercials, I’d just go back to the good ol' TV. But that’s not why I’m on the internet. I'm here to avoid those exact things.
And don’t even get me started on the *ad-blocker shaming*. Some websites claim that using an ad-blocker is like stealing. But you know what? If your entire business is predicated on tricking me into watching ads I don’t care about, **then maybe, just maybe, the problem isn’t with me, it’s with you**. *After all, no one likes being held hostage by a 30-second ad, except maybe the ad creators.* 
## Ad-Blockers: The Knights in Shining Armor
Speaking of ad-blockers: these things are the true heroes of the internet. *With just a few clicks*, you can reclaim the internet you were promised - the one without all the creepy pop-ups, auto-play videos, and sneaky ads disguised as ‘content’. It's like a breath of fresh air… *except the air doesn’t smell like stale advertising.* 
But it’s not just about the convenience, my friend. Ad-blockers also protect your privacy. They stop those **creepy** ads from following you around the internet, tracking every click you make, every website you visit, and probably even your private thoughts (but that’s coming, don’t worry). 
And, get this: **ad-blockers are actually doing advertisers a favor**. A study by the American Marketing Association revealed that ads are **190% more effective for ad-blocker non-users**. {color:#ff6347}[^3]{/color} Meaning, the people who aren’t using ad-blockers are actually the *ones* who are engaging with ads. So really, ad-blockers are just filtering out the people who have no interest in your irrelevant products. *You’re welcome, advertisers!*
## Conclusion
Let’s just stop pretending, shall we? Ads are not some necessary evil. They’re an annoyance, a distraction, and - frankly - a violation of online privacy. **We deserve better**, and with ad-blockers, we can have it. This isn’t just about the tech-savvy folk; it’s about *all of us* who are tired of being bombarded with unwanted ads every time we open our browsers. It’s about taking back control of our online experience and **making the internet a better place for everyone**, not just advertisers. 
So, if you're sick of the endless barrage of irrelevant ads, do yourself a favor and get an ad-blocker. Your sanity, and your privacy, will thank you.
---
### Sources:
**1)** [Ethan Cramer-Flood. "Worldwide Ad Spending Forecast 2025". 2025](https://www.emarketer.com/content/worldwide-ad-spending-forecast-2025)
**2)** [Blockthrough. "How Bad Ad Experiences Affect UX and Revenue for Publishers." Blockthrough, 2025.](https://blockthrough.com/blog/how-bad-ad-experiences-affect-ux-and-revenue-for-publishers/)
**3)** [American Marketing Association. "Research Insight | Ad Blockers Are an Opportunity, Not a Threat" American Marketing Association, 2023.](https://www.ama.org/research-insights/ad-blockers-are-an-opportunity-not-a-threat/)

title: Artificial Art: The Loss of Aesthetic Responsibility
content_raw:
# Artificial Art: The Loss of Aesthetic Responsibility
The art world stands on the brink of a dramatic **paradigm shift**, yet society refuses to embrace it. With the introduction of **artificial intelligence** as a "creative" tool, a new era begins that radically transforms the creation of art. What was once considered an exclusive, deeply human endeavor is now being taken over by **algorithms** and **machine learning**. Machines generate images, music, and texts at the push of a button, without a human ever picking up a paintbrush or pressing a key.
But what does this mean for the concept of art? And more importantly: what happens to **aesthetic responsibility** when machines take over the work once carried out by artists with **emotions**, **thoughts**, and **dedication**?  
Could it be that we are entering a new era of "*effortless excellence*" in AI art, where we forget the actual **effort** of creation and define ourselves only by the end product? And what becomes of human responsibility, which is so closely tied to the **creative force**? The answers to these questions go to the essence of what art truly is – and what it should remain.
---
## The Illusion of Democratic Art
Proponents of AI art often argue that it democratizes art. Finally, anyone can be an artist without having to practice for years. But this argument is deceptive. It overlooks the fact that art is not just a product but a **process**. A process shaped by **mistakes**, **doubts**, and **growth**. When we eliminate this process, we reduce art to a mere surface – something to consume, but no longer something we can truly experience.
The question is: what do we really gain when we remove the **effort** from art? Do we gain more beauty? More meaning? Or do we lose the ability to truly understand and appreciate art for what it is?
## AI: Not a Tool, But an "Implementation Automat"
First, we must make a fundamental distinction: **AI is not a tool**. A tool expands human abilities, helping us become better and take our creative visions to a new level. A brush or a camera are means of human creativity. They challenge the artist’s creativity and open new possibilities. AI, however, is not a tool, it is an **“implementation automat”** in some way. It completely replaces the creative effort and takes away the labor of actual creation. The human is no longer the creator of an artwork, but at best, the **client**. A comparison: If someone hires a **freelancer** to design a logo, they would rarely claim they created the logo themselves. The freelancer takes on the work and brings their expertise to the project. The client is not seen as the creator of the work. The same applies to AI.
Another comparison: When we talk about **Michelangelo** painting the Sistine Chapel, we don’t say that **Pope Julius II** painted the chapel simply because he commissioned it. The Pope was the **client**, Michelangelo the **creator**. The responsibility for creation rested with him. Similarly, one cannot claim today that the user of an AI system is the creator of an AI-generated "artwork." They merely give the direction, but the actual creation lies with an emotionless machine.
## Aesthetic Responsibility: A Loss of Depth
The concept of **aesthetic responsibility** is key when we talk about art. The creator of a piece of art bears responsibility for both the end product and the process that led to it. The significance of an artwork emerges from the artist’s engagement with the world, their **creativity**, their **vision**, and the **effort** they invest in their work. Michelangelo spent years working on the Sistine Chapel, pouring all his energy into the piece.
In AI, there is no such responsibility. AI has neither **intentions** nor an **awareness** of cultural or social contexts. It can recognize patterns and create replicas, but it doesn’t understand the significance of what it produces. An algorithm produces what it’s been taught, but it carries no responsibility for the aesthetic quality of its results. An illustrative example of this is the **infinite monkey theorem**: Theoretically, a monkey who types endlessly on a typewriter might eventually reproduce **Goethe’s Faust**. But even if this happened, it would not be the same as Goethe’s work. Why? Because the monkey lacks **intention**. It doesn’t understand what it’s doing. It has no **vision**, no **emotion**, no responsibility for what it creates. This is the exact same issue we face with AI art. The machine may replicate patterns, but it doesn’t understand what it’s doing. It has no **intention**, no **stance**, no artistic voice.
Why should we bother engaging with an artwork that no one ever has made the effort to create? Why should we admire a painting generated by a machine calculation when the “artist” has no real, personal responsibility for it? Art is a negotiation with one’s own abilities, limits, and vision. Those who seek the shortcut of AI bypass this process and lose the essence of art in doing so.
## The Myth of Effortless Creativity
There’s a dangerous illusion spreading in our society: the idea that **creativity** can happen without **effort**. That you simply input a **prompt**, and a masterpiece emerges. But this notion is not only wrong – it’s **destructive**. It devalues the work of all those who have practiced, experimented, and suffered for years to develop their craft.Art has always been tied to effort. Whether it’s the countless hours a musician spends practicing, the endless sketches a painter produces, or the many revisions a writer goes through... All of these efforts are not obstacles, but the path to true **creativity**. When we bypass this path, we lose not only the ability to create art but also the ability to appreciate it for what it truly is.
## AI Art = Democratic Art?
A frequently cited argument in the debate around AI art is the **democratization** of the creative process. The notion that anyone can now create art because all they need to do is type a few words into an AI system sounds appealing. But what this approach overlooks is the fact that real art is not just an end product, but a process, shaped by skill, practice, and effort.
It’s often said: “Music requires practice – that’s why we call it **music study**.” Herein lies the mistake when comparing AI use to **language learning**: If someone doesn’t speak **Spanish** but has never taken a Spanish course or practiced speaking, they can hardly be called “untalented.” On the contrary: They simply haven’t made the **human effort** to develop the necessary skills. Similarly, with AI art: if you let an AI generate artwork, you haven’t put in the effort required to create something truly **creative**. The AI does the work for you, but that doesn’t mean you’ve created a piece of art... you’ve merely issued a **command**.
## AI Art: A Flight from Responsibility?
Why are more and more people turning to AI to create art? Perhaps because it’s easier. Maybe because they believe the result “*is just good enough*" for us. But this is a profound **misconception**: art cannot be defined by the end product alone. What is lost in using AI as a creative tool is the responsibility for **craftsmanship** and **self-reflection** that true art demands.
It’s an indication that many no longer believe in their own **abilities**. They seek a way to be creative without the effort required for real art. But just like with learning a language or an instrument: you can’t expect to create a work of art unless you’re willing to put in the necessary work and practice. AI is a **convenience solution** for those who don’t want to go through the creative process.
## The Danger of Cultural Impoverishment
If we rely on AI to create art for us, we risk **cultural impoverishment**. Art is not just a product, but a dialogue between **creator** and **audience**. It challenges us, makes us think, moves us. When we abandon this dialogue, we lose a fundamental part of what makes us human.
Art is more than just **aesthetics**. It is a mirror of our society, an expression of our **values**, and a medium for **reflection**. If we delegate these functions to machines, we not only relinquish control over our culture, but we also lose the ability to understand ourselves.
---
## Conclusion: Art Remains an Act of Creation
In conclusion, **AI art** is a **facade** of creativity, but not real art. An artwork is more than just the end product: it’s the **process** behind it, the **effort**, the **thoughts**, and the **responsibility** of the creator. AI can take away the creative labor, but it can never replace the value of the artist’s own creation.If we start to view art only as a product without responsibility, we lose what it truly is: the deep, personal connection of the creator to their work and the dedication to the creative process. Art is more than just a surface to be reproduced. It’s a **journey** that humans undertake to understand themselves and the world around them.

{color:#ff5500}**The AI will never take this journey. And if we’re not careful, we may soon lose the ability to take it ourselves.**{/color}
---

author: Jackstar
title: Algorithmic Lobotomy: A Social Media Ban for teenagers
content_raw:
# Algorithmic Lobotomy: The Case for a Social Media Ban
The debate about a social media ban for 14 to 16-year-olds has become a *persistent* and *urgent* topic that just won't go away. It's almost amusing to see how seriously we discuss whether it's "*maybe a bit too much*" when teenagers spend up to eight hours a day watching 15-second clips. What do we expect from this? A journey of self-discovery through meaningless memes? A profound understanding of the humans through "*brainrot*"? The reality is far from enlightenment, because it's more like a collective fog of nonsense and digital garbage.
## Algorithmic Lobotomy: How Social Media is Eating Our Brains
You might think social media is the best place for young people to grow and thrive. But let's be honest, endlessly scrolling through *seamless perfect images* while their self-esteem drops faster than a lead balloon is not exactly inspiring. The effects on mental health are clear and worrying. The **American Psychological Association (APA)**{color:#ff6347}[^1]{/color} shows that too much social media use is linked to anxiety and severe depression. The constant need for likes creates a shaky sense of self that falls apart without constant approval and validation from random strangers.

Even worse is the loss of media literacy. It's painfully ironic for a generation known as  so called "*digital natives.*" Why bother with critical thinking or checking sources, when an algorithm conveniently feeds you information that matches your views and keeps you in your cozy little filter bubble? The result is a worrying lack of critical thinking skills. A study by the **Pew Research Center** shows that many young people struggle to tell facts from opinions or fake news, making them easy targets for conspiracy theories and extremist opinions{color:#ff6347}[^2]{/color}. We're raising a generation whose view of the world is shaped by a distorted, glamorous, and often false online bubble that has little to do with real world reality.
## From the Cradle to Brainrot: The Dumbing Down of a Generation
Before we even talk about 14-year-olds, let's take a good hard look at the younger kids. "*Brainrot*" isn't just an internet joke or a meme, it's rather a sad, disturbing reality. It's shocking that toddlers aged 2 or younger know the meaning of "*Skibidi Toilet*" and its nonsensical background before they can even articulate a proper sentence. This early digital overload is doing more than just wasting time, because it's leading to a kind of mental degeneration where imagination and creativity are lost in a sea of flashing lights and loud noises.

Studies in **JAMA Pediatrics** have shown that early screen time can seriously disrupt cognitive and language development in young children{color:#ff6347}[^3]{/color}. When developing brains are trained to expect quick and repeated dopamine hits from a young age, the ability to deal with the "boring" real world fades away. Isolated groups form, using their own incomprehensible language and becoming more disconnected from reality than ever before. It's like we're conducting a massive, unregulated experiment on our children's minds.
## The Education Paradox: Tutorials vs. TikTok Trance
Now, some people might argue that social media is a great educational tool. And they're partly right. I, for example, learned a lot of my technical skills and basically all my coding from online tutorials. These platforms do indeed offer a world of free knowledge to anyone who wants to learn. But - and this is a big but - it requires an enormous amount of self-discipline that most teenagers simply don't have. The algorithm doesn't care if you learn to anything, it wants you to watch the next viral prank or dance video, because it earns the platforms ways more ad-revenue. Educational content is "boring" for these platforms because it makes users think rather than just watch passively. Expecting teens to "learn the right thing" on social media is like putting a hungry child in a candy store and hoping they'll find the hidden vegetbles because it's good for them. It's simply never going to happen.
## The Myth of Parental Control: Who's Responsibility is it really?
When all else fails, people say, **"It's the parents' job!"** But this conveniently ignores the reality in most homes. Are parents really good examples of digital self-control? Often, they're just as hooked on their own feeds, mindlessly scrolling through their phones while their kids do the same. Parents are expected to manage their children's media use, but tech companies spend billions to make their products as addictive as possible. A government ban wouldn't be about limiting freedom, it would be about helping parents who can't possibly fight against the world's smartest algorithms, crafted by the brightest minds from deep inside the Silicon Valley. Who has the time and energy in a 40+-hour workweek to constantly monitor their kids' online activity? And even if they did, can they really compete with the sophisticated psychological manipulation built into these apps?
## Conclusion: A Ban as an Act of Reason and Responsibility
A social media ban for teenagers up to 14 or 16 years might seem extreme to some, but it's a necessary step if we care about the mental health, critical thinking skills, and real-life connections of our youth, and, more importantly, about our entire future as a mankind. Sure, communication might be disrupted for a while, but maybe, just maybe, teens will learn to have real conversations again. They might discover the joy of face-to-face interactions or the satisfaction of reading a book without constant notifications. It's time to wake them up from their digital daze and bring them back to reality. Because if we don't, we're not just failing our kids - we're failing our future as a society that values thought, reason, and genuine human connection.

The debate about a social media ban for 14 to 16-year-olds has become a persistent and urgent topic that just won't go away. It's almost amusing to see how seriously we discuss whether it's "maybe a bit too much" when teenagers spend up to eight hours a day watching 15-second clips. What do we expect from this? A journey of self-discovery through meaningless memes? A profound understanding of the humans through "brainrot"? The reality is far from enlightenment, because it's more like a collective fog of nonsense and digital garbage.

Algorithmic Lobotomy: How Social Media is Eating Our Brains

You might think social media is the best place for young people to grow and thrive. But let's be honest, endlessly scrolling through seamless perfect images while their self-esteem drops faster than a lead balloon is not exactly inspiring. The effects on mental health are clear and worrying. The American Psychological Association (APA)1 shows that too much social media use is linked to anxiety and severe depression. The constant need for likes creates a shaky sense of self that falls apart without constant approval and validation from random strangers.

Even worse is the loss of media literacy. It's painfully ironic for a generation known as so called "digital natives." Why bother with critical thinking or checking sources, when an algorithm conveniently feeds you information that matches your views and keeps you in your cozy little filter bubble? The result is a worrying lack of critical thinking skills. A study by the Pew Research Center shows that many young people struggle to tell facts from opinions or fake news, making them easy targets for conspiracy theories and extremist opinions2. We're raising a generation whose view of the world is shaped by a distorted, glamorous, and often false online bubble that has little to do with real world reality.

title: Behind the Scenes: How I Pretend to Know What I’m Doing
content_raw:
# Behind the Scenes: How I Pretend to Know What I’m Doing
Welcome, dear reader, to the **glamorous**, *high-stakes* world of blogging. Or at least, the world of my blogging, which is less "glamorous" and more "{color:#e67e22}me yelling into the void of the internet and occasionally getting a 'like' from my family.{/color}" If you’ve ever wondered how the sausage is made, how a half-baked idea transforms into a fully-baked (but still slightly raw in the middle) blog post, then **buckle up**. You’re about to get a backstage pass to the my idea factory, where dreams are born, opinions are shared, and AI does most of the heavy lifting (*or does it?* 🤔).
## Step 1: The Spark of Genius (Or Delusion)
Every great blog post starts with an idea. Sometimes that idea comes from me, the self-proclaimed "**expert**" in whatever topic I feel like ranting about that week. Other times, it comes from someone else who suggests a topic, and I think, "Sure, I can write about that!" - despite having only a surface-level ({color:#c0392b}**or no**{/color} 😅) understanding of it. But hey, that’s what Google, AI and a healthy dose of confidence are for.
 
If it’s an opinion piece, I sit down and think, "How can I make this sound like I know what I’m talking about?" **Spoiler alert:** I don’t always know what I’m talking about. But I’ve got kind of a skill for sounding like I do, which is really the important part. For fact-based stuff, like guides or reviews, I do what any self-respecting blogger would do: I scour the internet for pros and cons, find some sources that sound credible (or at least don’t sound completely made up), and then **pretend I’ve done thorough research**. It’s not lying, it’s *curating information*. There’s a difference, I swear. 😇

Once I’ve got my idea and a loose plan, I open up my **custom Markdown editor**. Yes, I built my own editor because, as a developer, I have an {color:#c0392b}unhealthy obsession with reinventing the wheel{/color}. Why use an existing tool when I can spend hours building something that does the same thing but with more bugs and less functionality? 
## Step 2: Drafting in German (Because Why Not?) 🇦🇹
Here’s where things get really fun. I draft my masterpiece in **German**. Why? Because I’m **Austrian** 🇦🇹, and it’s easier for me to sound smart in my native language. Plus, it gives me an excuse to use words like "_*Weltanschauung*_", "_*Schadenfreude*_" or "_*Rindfleischettikettierungsüberwachungsgesetz*_" without feeling pretentious. (Okay, maybe a little pretentious. 😏)

I’ve got templates for every type of article, sections for sources, headings, conclusions, because, let’s be honest, I’m not organized enough to remember all that stuff on my own. The template is basically my **blogging cheat sheet**, ensuring that I don’t forget to include things like "introduction" and "conclusion." Revolutionary stuff, I know. Now, if you’re thinking, "But Jackstar, isn’t writing in German and then translating it to English kind of extra work?" **Well, yes.** But it’s also a great way to ensure that readers from anywhere get the full experience of my thoughts, carefully curated and *{color:#7f8c8d}slightly lost in translation{/color}*. 
## Step 3: The Magic of AI (Or, How I Pretend to Be Fluent)
Once I’ve got my German draft, it’s time for the **real magic**: AI. I feed my draft into an AI (Mostly **MistralAI**), which does its best to turn my German gibberish into something resembling somewhat coherent English. Then, I go through and **tweak** the translation, making sure it sounds like something a *human* might actually write. Or at least, something I might write. This is also where I use AI to help me with grammar and spell-checking. Because, as much as I’d like to think I’m a grammatical genius, I’m really just a guy who knows how to use a computer (*I'm not even sure if I know that* 🤷‍♂️). And let’s face it, **AI is better at grammar than I am**. I mean, have you seen some of my blog posts? They read like they were written by a sleep-deprived teenager hopped up on caffeine. (Because they were.)

And yes, sometimes I use AI to help me arrange my thoughts. Is that cheating? **Maybe.** Do I care? **Not really**, because I'm transparent about it. If Shakespeare had access to AI, he probably would have used it too. Or maybe he’d have just stuck to writing sonnets about how much he hates his job. Who knows? 
## Step 4: Publishing
Once I’m happy with my post - or at least, happy enough to stop procrastinating - I hit "**publish**." But not just any publish. I use my **custom Flask dashboard** or Git to publish my posts. Because, again, why use an existing platform when I can build my own and then spend hours troubleshooting why it’s not working? My blog is hosted on *[Render.com’s free plan](https://render.com)*, because I’m cheap and I like free stuff. And let’s be honest, it’s not like I’m getting so much traffic that I need a premium plan. Yet. 

Here’s a little peek behind the curtain at how I add a blog post using **Flask**:
```python
def add_blog(new_blog: Dict[str, Any]) -> BlogPost:
    blogs: List[BlogPost] = load_blogs()
    now = int(time.time())
    if not new_blog.get("id"):
        existing_ids = {str(b.get("id")) for b in blogs}
        while (candidate := uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]) in existing_ids:
            pass
        new_blog["id"] = candidate
    else:
        new_blog["id"] = str(new_blog["id"])
    if isinstance(new_blog.get("author"), str):
        new_blog["author"] = [new_blog["author"]]
    raw_content = new_blog.get("content_raw", "")
    new_blog["content_html"] = MarkdownConverter.quick_convert(raw_content)
    new_blog.setdefault("tags", [])
    new_blog.setdefault("categories", [])
    new_blog.setdefault("time_created", now)
    new_blog["last_modified"] = now
    new_blog["calc_read_time"] = calculate_reading_time(new_blog["content_raw"])
    blogs.append(new_blog)
    saveand_refresh_cache(blogs)
    return new_blog
```
Look at that. It’s like *{color:#27ae60}poetry{/color}*, isn’t it? Or at least, it’s like something that makes sense to me and maybe a few other developers who also enjoy reinventing the wheel. 
## The Intentions: Why Do I Do This to Myself?
You might be wondering, "Jackstar, why do you put yourself through this?" Well, dear reader, it’s simple: **I love to write.** And by "love to write," I mean I enjoy the process of turning my **chaotic thoughts** into something that other people can read and (hopefully) understand. I started this blog because I wanted to share my opinions and educate people, especially on topics like **AI and IT**. Because, let’s face it, tech can be a bit of a black box for a lot of people. And I want to change that. Or at least, I want to make it a little less mysterious. But let’s be real, I also do it because I’m a bit of a **narcissist**. I like the idea of people reading my words and thinking, "Wow, Jackstar really knows his stuff!" Even if, half the time, I’m just winging it.
## The Struggles Are Real (And So Is My Sarcasm)
Blogging isn’t all fun and games. There are real struggles involved. Like **consistency**. Some weeks, I’m on fire, churning out post after post. Other weeks, I can barely string together a coherent sentence. But hey, that’s the way it is, right? *Right?* And don’t even get me started on **SEO**. It’s like trying to solve a Rubik’s Cube blindfolded, while also juggling flaming torches. And the torches are on fire. And the Rubik’s Cube is made of ice. You get the idea. But the biggest struggle? **Finding good sources.** There’s so much information out there, and half of it is either outdated, wrong, or written by someone who clearly doesn’t know what they’re talking about. (*Unlike me, of course.* 😉)
## The Personal Side: Me, Myself, and My Blog
So, what’s my routine like? Well, I mostly write on the **weekends**, when I have the time and energy to sit down and actually focus. But during the week, I’m constantly getting ideas and inspirations. Usually at the most inconvenient times, like when I’m trying to fall asleep and fix my sleep schedule. Because, you know, the brain likes to be difficult. 

And no, I don’t write to change minds or make money. I write because I’m **passionate** about it. And because I have a lot of opinions that I need to get off my chest. And because, let’s be honest, it’s a lot {color:#16a085}cheaper than therapy{/color} (Or is it, I don't know).
## What’s Next? (Or, How I Pretend to Have a Plan)
So, what’s next for my blog? Well, I’ve got **big plans**. Or at least, medium-sized plans. Maybe even small plans. **Micro-plans** are better than no plans, I suppose. I’m thinking about adding guest posts and interviews. Because, let’s face it, my opinions can only carry this blog so far. Plus, it’ll be nice to have someone else do the writing for a change. 

But for now, I’ll keep doing what I’m doing: **writing, ranting, and occasionally pretending to know what I’m talking about**. And if you’ve got any suggestions for what I should write about next, feel free to let me know. Or don’t. I’m not your boss. 
---
## Closing Thoughts: The End (Or Is It?)
And there you have it, folks. The behind-the-scenes look at how this blog comes to life. It’s a mix of **passion**, **sarcasm**, and a whole lot of **irony**. But hey, it works. Mostly. 
Thanks for reading, and remember: **if you ever feel like you don’t know what you’re doing, just pretend you do**. It’s worked for me so far. Until next time, stay curious, stay sarcastic, and keep yelling into the void of the internet. Someone out there is listening. *Probably.*
---

title: Digital Slavery 2.0: We Work for Tech Giants - Without Knowing It and Without Getting Paid
content_raw:
# Digital Slavery 2.0: We Work for Tech Giants - Without Knowing It and Without Getting Paid
In the modern digital world, there is a new form of forced labor: unconscious work for tech giants. While we think we're spending our free time online, we are actually working for companies like Amazon, Google, and Facebook - all, **without getting paid** for it.
## The Invisible Workers of the Digital World
Behind the amazing capabilities of Artificial Intelligence (AI) lies one of the most insidious forms of modern forced labor: *the human labor necessary to ensure that no human labor is needed anymore*, the work that lets machines "learn." AI systems require data that must first be made "readable" for the machines. This work is done by people who are often paid **below the subsistence level**. These "invisible workers" are the true cogs in the AI machinery, ensuring that it functions.
In countries with low wages or precarious working conditions, millions of people are involved in this invisible work. The problem: the pay for these "tasks" is so low, it barely covers living expenses.

But even in Western countries, this form of work is not uncommon. So-called "data annotators" work in call centers and under extremely poor working conditions to ensure the data used to train AI systems is correct. And while these people toil, the tech giants make a fortune, without ever seeing the "workers" they need for their success.{color:#ff6347}[^2]{/color}
## Everyone Contributes Unpaid Labor to These Corporations
But it’s not just these people who work for the tech giants, because actually, **everyone** is involved: Every click, every interaction on a website or app is **actually unpaid labor** for the tech giant that operates it. You are a **data generator**, providing hours of work for an invisible machine that you don’t even see. Your time on TikTok is monetized through advertising revenue, while you think you’re just consuming a few videos ([*which I actually **also advise against**, but that’s another topic*](/blog/818fe5bb)). On YouTube and Netflix, you’re not a viewer, but a worker, unconsciously helping to improve algorithms and boost advertising revenue. And what do you get for it? **Nothing**. No money, no recognition, just more and more ads. (more about that in [another article](/blog/f63e1c40))
## Conclusion: Who Is Actually Working Here?
We all do it, without realizing it. The tech giants earn billions while we "work" for them by generating and improving data, which we think of as the "user experience." {color:#ff6347}Maybe it’s time to ask: **Who really benefits and who pays the price?**{/color}

title: The Feedback Loop of Nothingness: Navigating the Era of Eloquent Nonsense
content_raw:
# The Feedback Loop of Nothingness: Navigating the Era of Eloquent Nonsense
Welcome to an era where we have collectively decided that **"letting others think for us"** is the new **"thinking for ourselves"**. When one enters the digital world today, one feels trapped in a giant advertising event for the next evolutionary (or perhaps *regressional*) stage of humanity. Everywhere we look, the acronym **"AI"** jumps at us, as if it were the Holy Grail that finally eliminates the tedious necessity of using our own brains for the simplest tasks like writing an email or solving a trivial problem. We find ourselves in a phase of total AI obsession, where the line between technological breakthrough and absurd marketing hype has become so thin that it can barely be seen with the naked eye (or a neural network). But before we dive deeper into this glittering world of artificial intelligence, we must turn to a historical parallel that is more relevant today than ever.
## A Detour into History: The Birth of Snake Oil
The term *(digital) "snake oil"* is ubiquitous in the tech world today, but its roots lie in the Wild West of the 19th century.
Back then, shady figures like Clark Stanley, the self-proclaimed **"Rattlesnake King"**, traveled through the country selling bottles of tincture allegedly made from rattlesnakes. This brew was supposed to help with almost everything: from rheumatism to toothaches to baldness (a claim that would probably be repackaged by an AI startup today as a **"personalized hair regeneration model"**). The bitter reality came to light in 1917 when the US government examined the contents and found that while it contained mineral oil, beef tallow, and red pepper, there wasn't a single molecule of snake in it.{color:#ff6347}[^1]{/color} Stanley had simply stolen the reputation of the Chinese water snake, whose oil actually contains anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, and replaced it with cheap industrial gunk.

Today, digital snake oil can be found everywhere companies slap **"AI"** on their products, even though under the hood there's merely a simple Excel spreadsheet or a rigid chain of *if-then statements*. It's the art of turning mediocre software into a revolutionary marvel of modern technology by sticking on a shiny label.
## The Great Label Swindle: Algorithm vs. AI
It's fascinating to observe how quickly we've adapted our language to please the marketing departments of the world. In the past, an algorithm was a clear thing: a precise sequence of instructions that generates an output from an input (like a recipe for a cake, only the computer doesn't accidentally forget the baking powder).

A classic algorithm is **logical**, **predictable**, and above all, **transparent**. If the program crashes, you can usually tell exactly which line caused the error. But suddenly, any software capable of sorting data or recognizing a pattern is dubbed **"Artificial Intelligence"**. The difference, however, is fundamental.

Genuine modern AI, particularly in the field of machine learning, is not based on rigid rules but on **statistical probabilities**. But because **"AI-powered"** sounds so much more impressive in a quarterly report than *"we conducted a statistical analysis"*, the label is inflationarily stuck on everything that produces an output from more than two variables. This leads consumers to believe that their washing machine is making **"intelligent decisions"** when, in reality, it's just weighing the laundry (something it could do twenty years ago, by the way).
## The Stochastic Parrots and Their Chatter
When we talk about ChatGPT or similar Large Language Models (LLMs), we need to lift the veil of magic that Silicon Valley has so carefully woven around these systems. An LLM is not a digital philosopher or an all-knowing god.

At its core, it's an extremely advanced version of the autocompletion on your smartphone, trained with the entire text mass of the internet. The functionality can be reduced to the principle of **"Next-Token-Prediction"**: The model mathematically calculates which word component (token) is most likely to follow next, based on the billions of sentences it has *"read"* during training.{color:#ff6347}[^2]{/color} When you ask it how it's doing, it doesn't respond out of feeling but because the statistical probability for the answer sequence *"I'm doing well, thank you for asking"* is extremely high in its dataset.

Scientists have coined the apt term **"stochastic parrots"** for this phenomenon.{color:#ff6347}[^3]{/color} These systems imitate language without ever understanding the meaning behind it. They're like a parrot living in a lecture hall for theoretical physics, eventually chattering along so perfectly that you might mistake it for a professor, until you ask it why it's speaking, and it responds with just a blank stare.
## Why the Comparison with the Internet Falls Short
Often, the argument is made that with every new technology - be it the internet, smartphones, or calculators - we've heard the same doomsday chorus. *"People will forget how to calculate,"* they said about calculators. *"The internet will kill libraries,"* they said in the nineties. And yet, humanity has survived and adapted. But AI is not just a tool for accessing information like the internet.

The internet merely made access to knowledge easier; it was the infrastructure, a kind of digital postman. AI, on the other hand, is the first attempt to automate the cognitive processing of this knowledge. For the first time in history, we have a technology that not only supports human intelligence and behavior but widely imitates it. A calculator computes the square root of a number, but it doesn't write your term paper on the causes of the Thirty Years' War (and it doesn't claim with utter conviction that Napoleon was actually an avid surfer). The danger with AI is that we're not just delegating a task but handing over the process of thinking and judging itself to a black box whose decision-making bases we often don't understand even in the slightest.
## When It Makes Sense: Tool Instead of Oracle
Despite all the justified skepticism and opinions of cynics (of which I am one), there are areas where the use of this technology is not only sensible but downright liberating.

The key is not to see AI as an oracle that knows the truth but as a kind of high-speed secretariat or inspiration engine. Anyone sitting in front of a blank document with no idea how to start can receive valuable impulses from an AI draft to overcome their writer's block.

The technology is particularly strong when we dictate the **context to it completely**. If I give the system my own notes, research, and facts and say: **"Structure this for me"**, then the AI functions as an excellent editor. In this closed system, the danger of hallucinations (i.e., the moment when the AI starts making things up with great confidence) decreases because it doesn't have to generate facts from *"nothing"* but works on the provided prompt.

It's like a hammer: you can build a house with it or hit your thumb. The problem is that currently, many people are trying to eat soup with a hammer. *(Or something like that)*
---
## The Creeping Atrophy of the Mind - Or: Why We're Unlearning How to Think
One of the most subtle but dangerous risks of current AI development is **cognitive atrophy**, a creeping muscle wasting of the mind. We've already seen a generation that can't find a supermarket two streets away without Google Maps because their sense of orientation has been outsourced to a blue dot on the screen.

With generative AI, we're taking this outsourcing process to the next level: we're now delegating the structuring of thoughts and the formulation of arguments, or even having them invented entirely. We must realize that writing and arguing aren't just about recording and presenting results. Writing is the process of thinking itself. While we painstakingly search for the right word or try to squeeze a complex issue into a logical sentence, we force our brain to perform at its best. We check contradictions, weigh information, and sharpen our own opinion. If we eliminate this **"cognitive resistance"** by asking AI to *"quickly summarize something"* or *"formulate a convincing argument"*, we rob ourselves of the exercise that makes us critical thinkers in the first place *(a toast to convenience, which makes us as intellectually flexible as a slice of toast bread in the long run)*.

This phenomenon is particularly evident in education and the workplace. When students have their essays written by AI, they don't just miss out on the text but also the intellectual engagement with the topic. They no longer learn how to synthesize information or develop their own voice. In the professional world, too, blind trust in AI output leads us to overlook nuances. Because the models formulate so eloquently and politely, we tend to question their output less critically than the text of a real person *(who might make spelling mistakes or seem unsympathetic)*.

This **"manipulation through eloquence"**, as I like to call it, is a real danger because the likelihood increases that we unconsciously adopt logical errors or factual nonsense simply because the tone sounds so authoritative and flawless. When the machine speaks with the authority of a professor, hardly anyone questions whether it's just calculating the statistical probability of nonsense. We risk becoming mere **"prompt pushers"** who know which button to press but have no idea what's happening in the machine room.
## The Paradox of Emptiness
We're heading towards a scenario that I like to call the **"Great Irony Dilemma of Modernity"**. Imagine an everyday office life that is already partially a reality today: An employee uses AI to generate a detailed, five-page email from three bullet points because it looks *"more professional"* (and they don't feel like writing anyway). The recipient of this email gets this digital wall of text but, faced with their own flood of emails, has no time or desire to read it. So they also use AI to summarize the text back into three bullet points. In this cycle, an enormous amount of computing power and electricity has been consumed{color:#ff6347}[^4]{/color}, only to end up with exactly the same information that was there at the beginning. The net gain in information is zero *(or even negative, as things tend to get lost in all this "prompt pushing")*, but both parties feel incredibly efficient and productive, even though they've only participated in an artificial inflation of reality.
## The "Dead Internet": When Bots Write for Bots
Closely linked to this emptiness is the so-called **"Dead Internet Theory"**. What used to sound like a paranoid conspiracy theory now feels like a frighteningly accurate prediction. The theory states that the internet died years ago and is now almost exclusively inhabited by bots that create AI-generated content to influence other bots or manipulate search engine rankings. If you look at platforms like X or the comment sections of major news sites today, you increasingly encounter **"zombie content"**: interchangeable, soulless texts that only serve to place keywords. The problem is that AI models now start feeding on the data that other AIs have already published on the internet *(pardon the wording, but **"digital incest"** describes it almost better)*.

Scientists warn here of **"model collapse"**: when an AI is trained with AI-generated data, it starts to amplify errors and lose its own variance until the result is nothing but incomprehensible gibberish.{color:#ff6347}[^5]{/color}

We're flooding the net with SEO-optimized garbage written for algorithms, not humans. This makes it increasingly difficult to find genuine human experiences, original thoughts, or even reliable sources. The internet is transforming from a global knowledge repository into an echo chamber of insignificance, where AIs confirm each other's hallucinations while the human user frustratingly tries to find a single genuine experience report among three million AI-generated product reviews *(which was probably also written by a bot to cash in on commissions)*.
## Censorship, Bias, and the Silicon Valley Filter
Another often underrated risk is the creeping influence on our societal opinion landscape through the training data of the models. An AI is never neutral - it's a reflection of the data it's been fed and the filters its creators have imposed on it. Since most large models are developed in Silicon Valley, they inevitably carry the cultural and moral values of that region. This leads to a kind of **"censorship of the average"**, where controversial or complex opinions are often suppressed in favor of a smoothed-over, politically correct, or simply Western perspective.

AI thus becomes a tool for subtle manipulation that doesn't work through prohibitions but through statistical weighting. Those who ask the model for historical or political assessments often get the *"safest"* answer, which can lead to a flattening of discourse in the long run. When the machine decides what's *"appropriate"*, we give up a piece of our cultural sovereignty to private tech companies.
## The Democratization of Fraud
Finally, we must not forget the dark side of the coin: the massive increase in scams and spam. In the past, it was relatively easy to recognize a fraudulent email - poor grammar and absurd content were the warning signs. Thanks to AI, scammers can now compose perfect, personalized messages in any language in seconds, so convincing that even experienced users fall for them. We're being flooded with a torrent of generic content designed solely to steal attention or extract money. The **"dead internet"**, where bots have taken over, is no longer a dystopian fairy tale but a real threat to the integrity of our digital communication.
---
## Conclusion: The Mouth Without Knowledge
In conclusion, we can certainly describe ChatGPT and its ilk as an eloquently chattering mouth without any real knowledge behind it. The danger lies less in the technology itself than in the enthusiastic volume of those who sell it as a panacea without understanding the statistical nature of these systems.

We should use AI for what it is: a fascinating, highly efficient tool for specific tasks that can take work off our hands but never our thinking. Those who rely on digital snake oil and believe they can solve all cognitive problems with it will find in the end that they've produced a lot of text, but the essence of understanding has been lost. **Stay critical**, question the *"magical"* promises, and remember that true intelligence is more than just predicting the next word.

{color:#ff6347}*(Written with the quiet hope that no bot will use this text to generate a "better" version of it.)*{/color}
---
### Sources:
**Thumbnail: ** Adapted and modified from an image generated with Gemini (Nano Banana 2)
**1)** [Haynes, A. (2015). The history of snake oil.](https://pharmaceutical-journal.com/article/opinion/the-history-of-snake-oil)
**2)** [Wolfram, S. (2023). What Is ChatGPT Doing ... and Why Does It Work? Stephen Wolfram Writings.](https://writings.stephenwolfram.com/2023/02/what-is-chatgpt-doing-and-why-does-it-work/)
**3)** [Bender, E. M., Gebru, T., et al. (2021). On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3442188.3445922)
**4)** [Li, P., et al. (2023). Making AI Less "Thirsty": Uncovering and Addressing the Secret Water Footprint of AI Models.](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.03271)
**5)** [Shumailov, I., et al. (2024). AI models collapse when trained on recursively generated data. Nature Journal.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07566-y)

The "dead internet", where bots have taken over, is no longer a dystopian fairy tale but a real threat to the integrity of our digital communication.

Conclusion: The Mouth Without Knowledge

In conclusion, we can certainly describe ChatGPT and its ilk as an eloquently chattering mouth without any real knowledge behind it. The danger lies less in the technology itself than in the enthusiastic volume of those who sell it as a panacea without understanding the statistical nature of these systems.

We should use AI for what it is: a fascinating, highly efficient tool for specific tasks that can take work off our hands but never our thinking. Those who rely on digital snake oil and believe they can solve all cognitive problems with it will find in the end that they've produced a lot of text, but the essence of understanding has been lost. Stay critical, question the "magical" promises, and remember that true intelligence is more than just predicting the next word.

(Written with the quiet hope that no bot will use this text to generate a "better" version of it.)

title: The Deluge of Characters: The Reading Paradox of Generation Alpha
content_raw:
#The Deluge of Characters: The Reading Paradox of Generation Alpha
We are currently witnessing a bizarre, historical contradiction: Never before in human history has a generation "read" so many words per day as Generation Alpha (born approx. 2010 onwards). They are literally swimming in an endless ocean of TikTok captions, YouTube comments, WhatsApp messages, and fleeting subtitles. Yet, while this generation is drowning in an unprecedented quantity of text, they are starving for the quality of comprehension. **What good is the world's fully stocked library in your pocket if the key to meaning has been lost at the bottom of this digital data ocean?** 

The following text analyzes the alarming cognitive capitulation of an entire generation - a development that goes far beyond a mere reading weakness and threatens the very foundations of independent thought. 
##The Status Quo: Diagnoses of Intellectual Decline
The hard, empirical facts of educational research now read like an obituary for classical literacy. Verified data from the **PIRLS (IGLU) 2021 study** (published 2023) to grasp the absolute gravity of the situation: In Germany and large parts of the DACH region (including your home country, Austria), **a quarter ({color:#E74C3C}25%{/color}) of all children** now leave primary school without having reached the minimum competency level in reading comprehension.[^1] They can look at words, but they cannot understand them.

A glance across the Atlantic reveals a downright educational disaster: The current US data from the **National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP) 2024** shows that reading scores have plummeted to their lowest level in decades, with already lower-performing students sliding even more dramatically.[^2] 

What is massively collapsing here is not literary flair, but fundamental mechanics. It fails at the absolute basics:
{indent}- **The Ruins of Letter Recognition (Grapheme-Phoneme Correspondence):** The ability to fluently translate what is seen into a mental sound no longer functions. 
- **Articulation Aphasia and Missing Prosody:** He who has to decode every single letter loses the rhythm. Without the correct intonation, the meaning of a sentence disintegrates into a pile of disjointed sounds.{/indent}
> **The Comparison:** It surprised absolutely no one that the competence of reading comprehension is missing when fundamental things like letter recognition are not automated. After all, this is like a baker preheating the oven to **180°C** to bake a cake, without possessing even a single gram of flour or sugar. Or like sitting in front of a bombastic next-gen video game on an 8K monitor - only **without a controller**. The image is there, the system is running, but any interaction with the content is physically impossible.

##The Illusion of "Reading a Lot": Quantity Kills Quality
"But they read all day long!" - this argument is a dangerous, digital fallacy. Consuming tens of thousands of words daily in the form of social media snippets is akin to trying to survive on cotton candy: It fills the mouth, but it provides absolutely no nourishment. 

Digital texts are ephemeral, extremely compressed, and mostly accompanied by glaring visual stimuli. The brain is trained for **vacuous scrolling and fleeting scanning**. While true, immersive reading ("Deep Reading") builds neural pathways for empathy, frustration tolerance, and complex logic, the constant skimming of comments degrades the brain to a mere instantaneous water heater for banal sequences of letters. Information is briefly captured, but it never sinks into the long-term memory.
##Root Causes: A Toxic Mix of Crisis and Consumption
Why is a generation forgetting how to read in the midst of the information age? It is a catastrophic cocktail of several factors that mutually amplify each other:
{indent}- **The Pandemic Catalyst (Covid-19):** School closures were a death blow for literacy. Learning to read requires extremely precise, phonological instruction and direct correction by teachers in the early years. Learning in front of a home screen led to an unprecedented, globally measurable "Learning Loss" that is still reflected in NAEP and PIRLS data today.[^3]
- **The Silent Tragedy of "iPad Babies":** Cognitive corrosion begins long before school. Babies who are strapped motionless in a stroller and glued to a smartphone no longer experience an analog world accompanied by language. The early sensory overload from screens replaces the interactive, human dialogue that is essential for building the brain's language center.[^4]
- **{color:#F39C12}7.5 Hours{/color} of Digital Trance:** According to current evaluations, the average screen time of 6- to 18-year-olds is an unfathomable **{color:#F39C12}7.5 hours per day{/color}**.[^5] In these seven and a half hours, absolutely zero cognitive effort takes place. It is a passive bombardment that systematically hollows out the brain's attention center and drops the frustration tolerance for "slow" media (like a printed book) to absolute zero.{/indent}
##The End of Independent Thought? The AI Trap
As if these omens weren't precarious enough, generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) now enters the scene as a lethal accelerant. For many students, AI increasingly acts as an **intellectual wheelchair**. Why endure the arduous task of reading and structuring when ChatGPT summarizes a text in three seconds? 

Here the trap snaps shut: When the thinking process-the laborious wrestling with texts, extracting meaning, and drawing one's own conclusions-is outsourced to algorithms, the human "thinking muscle" atrophies. Information can no longer be remembered because it was never neurologically processed. The AI solves the problem before the child's brain has even understood the conflict. Thus, we unwittingly breed a generation of users who can read the output of a machine, but are no longer capable of verifying its truthfulness or ethical depth.
##Conclusion: An Analog Wake-Up Call
We stand at the crossroads of cultural evolution. Generation Alpha is not stupider than previous generations, but they are systematically **under-challenged and digitally overfed** by a toxic media environment. If we do not radically change course and put the fundamental, analog ingredients of reading (silence, endurance, concentration, and conscious letter recognition) back into focus, we risk the highest good of our civilization: Free, critical thought. Because he who does not master the language is mastered by the language-or in the 21st century: by the algorithm.

U.S. Department of Education.](https://nces.ed.gov/use-work/resource-library/report/statistical-analysis-report/2024-naep-reading-assessment-results-grades-4-and-8-nation-states-and-districts)
**3)** [OECD (2022/2024): The Impact of COVID-19 on Education – International Evidence. OECD Publishing.](https://web-archive.oecd.org/pdfViewer?path=/2020-09-08/562941-the-impact-of-covid-19-on-education-insights-education-at-a-glance-2020.pdf)
**4)** [Madigan, S. et al. (2020): Association Between Screen Time and Children's Performance on a Developmental Screening Test. JAMA Pediatrics.](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamapediatrics/fullarticle/2722666)
